$description: Shared design foundations for the Yogananda digital ecosystem. These tokens express calm technology principles (PRI-08), accessibility requirements (PRI-07), and the 10-year design horizon (PRI-10). They apply regardless of organization (SRF or YSS). Organization-specific tokens override or extend these.

space.$description: Spacing rhythm. Base unit 8px. Generous by default — digital silence is whitespace (ADR-065). Never cramped; the teachings deserve breathing room.

tight.$description: Minimum. Inline icon gaps, tight label spacing.

compact.$description: Dense UI: search results list, settings rows.

default.$description: Standard gap between elements. Comfortable without being lavish.

generous.$description: Reading content margins. The default generosity of this design language.

spacious.$description: Section padding. Distinct regions of content.

expansive.$description: Major section separation. The digital equivalent of a page turn.

vast.$description: Page-level breathing. Hero spacing. The portal threshold.

motion.$description: All motion is functional, never decorative (PRI-08). Maximum 0.3s for standard UI transitions. Contemplative transitions may be slower — they serve spiritual pacing, not decoration. prefers-reduced-motion: reduce → instant all transitions.

instant.$description: No transition. State changes that must feel immediate (toggle, dismiss).

interaction.$description: Hover, focus, click response. Fast enough to feel instant, slow enough to be smooth.

org.yogananda.design.evaluationTrigger: If users report lag, reduce to 100ms.

content.$description: Content appearing or disappearing. Theme switches, panel reveals, result loading. The maximum for standard UI.

org.yogananda.design.rationale: 0.3s is the calm technology ceiling for UI transitions.

contemplative.$description: Sacred pacing. Chapter transitions (DES-012 'Breath Between Chapters'), portal threshold (DES-007), dwell activation. Content worthy of Yogananda's words deserves unhurried transitions.

arrival.$description: Portal entry. The Opening Moment (DES-007). Slow enough to feel like crossing a threshold. The slight delay is intentional — the portal pauses before greeting.

org.yogananda.design.rationale: Entering a sacred space is not instantaneous.

easing.$description: Named easing curves. All use cubic-bezier.

standard.$description: Material-inspired standard easing. Most UI transitions.

decelerate.$description: Elements entering the screen. Arrives quickly, settles slowly.

accelerate.$description: Elements leaving the screen. Departs slowly, exits quickly.

contemplative.$description: Gentle, unhurried. For sacred content transitions.

touch.$description: Touch target requirements. Non-negotiable accessibility minimums (PRI-07, WCAG 2.5.8). ~70% of Hindi/Spanish audience is mobile-first (ADR-128).

target-min.$description: Minimum interactive touch target. Buttons, links, toggles. No exceptions.

target-comfortable.$description: Preferred touch target. Primary actions, frequently tapped elements.

reading.$description: Reading experience parameters. These govern how the portal presents text — the core activity of the entire ecosystem.

settled-debounce.$description: How long a seeker dwells on a paragraph before the portal responds with related teachings. 1.2 seconds of stillness. The portal waits; it does not interrupt.

org.yogananda.design.evaluationTrigger: User testing Milestone 3a. Shorter = more responsive but interrupts reading.

focus-zone.$description: Percentage of viewport top/bottom to exclude from focus detection. Middle 30% is the 'focus zone' — paragraphs here are candidates for settled paragraph. Value represents the margin percentage.

org.yogananda.design.evaluationTrigger: Wider margin = more focused detection. Narrower = more sensitive.

line-length.$description: Optimal line length per script. Based on typographic research for comfortable sustained reading.

latin.$description: Optimal English/Spanish line length in characters (65-75 range). Bringhurst standard. ~42em at body size.

devanagari.$description: Optimal Hindi line length in aksharas (40-50 range). ADR-080. Devanagari glyphs are wider; fewer characters per line maintains rhythm.

breakpoint.$description: Responsive breakpoints. Mobile-first progressive enhancement (PRI-05, PRI-07). A seeker in rural Bihar on 2G and a seeker in Los Angeles on fiber both get the complete experience.

md.$description: Tablets portrait. Suggestion count changes here.

lg.$description: Tablets landscape, small desktops. Side panel appears.

xl.$description: Full desktop. Presentation mode enlarges further.

radius.$description: Border radius. Soft but not bubbly — a library, not a playground.

none.$description: Sharp corners. Reading surfaces, content blocks.

small.$description: Subtle rounding. Keyboard focus indicators, hover highlights.

pill.$description: Fully rounded. Buttons, tags, badges. SRF's established interaction pattern from yogananda.org.

shadow.$description: Shadows are minimal. The design language prefers borders and background shifts over drop shadows. When shadows appear, they are subtle and warm.

subtle.$description: Barely perceptible elevation. Floating elements that need just a hint of lift.

elevated.$description: Modals, dropdown menus, floating panels. The most elevation this design language uses.

opacity.$description: Named opacity levels for consistent transparency across the system.

ghost.$description: Paper texture noise. The ghost of physicality.

whisper.$description: Paragraph hover backgrounds. Felt, not seen.

subdued.$description: Dimmed paragraphs in dwell mode. Background content.

secondary.$description: Secondary text, scroll indicators. Supporting role.

primary.$description: Primary secondary text. Readable but not dominant.

full.$description: Full opacity. Primary text, interactive elements.

crimson.$description: Publication crimson. The structural voice of the printed book — chapter titles, section dividers, drop capitals. Extracted from the SRF Autobiography of a Yogi Kindle edition (2025). Used when rendering content from published SRF/YSS books to create visual continuity between physical and digital reading. Etymology: Sanskrit kṛmi-ja (कृमिज) → Arabic qirmiz → English crimson. A word of Indian origin that became the Western bookmaking standard for rubricated chapter headings — the bridge between the two traditions this design system serves.

org.yogananda.design.rationale: SRF chose this crimson for their most sacred text. In the printed AoY, crimson serves exactly three structural roles: chapter numbers, chapter titles, and section dividers. It never colors body text. This constraint carries forward into digital: crimson marks the book's skeleton, not its flesh. Shared across organizations because AoY is published by both SRF and YSS.
org.yogananda.design.etymology: The word 'crimson' comes from Sanskrit kṛmi-ja (कृमिज, 'produced by a worm') — the kermes insect dye. The word traveled from Sanskrit through Arabic (qirmiz) to Old Spanish to English. A word of Indian origin that became the standard for Western fine bookmaking. It is, literally, the bridge between the two traditions this design system serves.
org.yogananda.design.cultural-lineage: This is the tradition of rubrication — from Latin rubrica (red earth) — chapter headings in red, practiced in Western manuscripts since the 4th century. SRF's choice of crimson for their most sacred text connects to this millennium-old bookmaking practice while simultaneously echoing Indian devotional red (kumkum, sindoor, the color of Shakti).
org.yogananda.design.evaluationTrigger: If SRF publishes a new AoY edition with different accent colors, or if SRF provides an official Pantone/hex specification that differs from this extraction.

crimson-opacity.$description: Crimson at calibrated attention levels for publication surfaces. Five levels — intentionally fewer than gold's six. Crimson marks structural hierarchy (chapter titles, dividers, drop caps) but has no 'texture' level because it serves structure, not materiality: there is no crimson paper texture, only crimson structure atop gold atmosphere. The ambient (0.25) and highlight (0.15) values are lower than gold's (0.3, 0.2) because crimson (#9B2335) is perceptually heavier — darker and more saturated than gold (#dcbd23). The same opacity level would feel MORE prominent in crimson. Lower values maintain equivalent felt-weight across hues. This principle — hue-dependent opacity calibration — applies wherever accent colors differ: YSS terracotta similarly reduces gold-highlight from 0.2 to 0.12.

interactive.$description: Chapter titles, section dividers, publication navigation. Full presence — the book's structure declares itself.

decorative.$description: Drop capitals, ornamental chapter marks. The illuminated manuscript tradition — beautiful, not calling.

ambient.$description: Book progress indicator, chapter markers in table of contents. Peripheral structural awareness.

highlight.$description: Hover on chapter navigation, active chapter in sidebar. Guiding through the book's structure.

subliminal.$description: Background warmth on publication-specific surfaces. The faintest breath of red — like the binding glimpsed in peripheral vision.

z-index.$description: Stacking context. Flat hierarchy — avoid z-index wars.

top.$description: Scroll indicator, toast notifications. Always visible.
